analysis.note: Predicate with asau. Sthiramati explains it as absence of grāhaka-citta, so the sense is not simply “unconscious” or “inconceivable.”
Bhāṣya verse reading has -lamba-; Sthiramati’s prose gloss/requotation uses the regular anupalambha- form. The meaning is fixed as non-apprehension of the grāhya-artha.
Masculine deictic, construed with acittaḥ and anupalambaḥ; rendered by English “it/that.”
Neuter nominative predicate with tat; the form is identical with accusative singular.
Agrees with jñānam/tat; sandhi shows final -m as homorganic ñ before ca.

verification.detail: compound stem lokottara as Napumsaka/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Enclitic conjunction joining this predication to the preceding predicates.
Neuter nominative deictic referring to the jñāna just characterized.
Genitive dependent on parāvṛttiḥ: “turning-about of the basis.” Sthiramati defines the basis here as sarvabījaka ālayavijñāna.
Subject/predicative term; Sthiramati supplies bhavati and says it occurs after that lokottara jñāna.
Printed continuously with the following in the prompt; Sthiramati isolates dvidhā and applies it to the two obscuration-dauṣṭhulyas.
Ablatival tasil form: “from/through the abandonment of dauṣṭhulya.” The preceding dvidhā specifies the dauṣṭhulya, not a regular feminine ablative hānyāḥ.

verification.detail: avyayībhāva; whole form not in kosha/MW as indeclinable

justifications.decision: Translate acitta not as “inconceivable” or merely “unconscious,” but as lacking grasper-mind; translate anupalamba as non-apprehension of the grāhya object.
options_considered: acitta as inconceivable/unthought
acitta as simply unconscious or inanimate
acitta as absence of grāhaka-citta, with anupalamba as absence of apprehension of grāhya-artha
justifications.chosen: acitta = absence of apprehender-mind; anupalamba = non-apprehension of the grasped object

evidence.translation: Because of the absence of apprehender-mind and because of non-apprehension of the object-to-be-grasped, ‘that is acitta and anupalambha.’

justifications.decision: Render lokottara as “world-transcending/supramundane,” not merely “extraordinary,” because Sthiramati derives it from having crossed beyond the world through non-worldly, non-conceptual occurrence.
options_considered: lokottara as merely unusual or extraordinary
lokottara as superior to worldly things in a loose sense
lokottara as beyond the world because nirvikalpa and not occurring as worldly practice

evidence.quote:
anucitatvāl loke samudācārābhāvān nirvikalpatvāc ca lokād uttīrṇam iti
jñānaṃ lokottarañ ca tad iti
evidence.translation: Because it is not customary, because it does not occur in the world, and because it is non-conceptual, it has crossed beyond the world; therefore, ‘that is supramundane knowledge.’

justifications.decision: Take jñānam, lokottaram, and tat as nominatives: ‘that is supramundane knowledge,’ not as an accusative object.
options_considered: jñānam as accusative object of an implied verb
jñānam/lokottaram/tat as neuter nominative predicate expression
justifications.chosen: jñānam lokottaram ca tat is a neuter nominative predication

evidence.quote:
jñānaṃ lokottarañ ca tad iti
tasya jñānasyānantaraṃ āśrayasya parāvṛttir bhavatīti jñāpanārtham āha
evidence.translation: ‘And that is supramundane knowledge’; to make known that after that knowledge the turning-about of the basis occurs, he says ...

justifications.decision: Translate āśrayasya parāvṛttiḥ as the turning-about of the basis, with the basis specifically identified as the all-seed ālaya-vijñāna, and construe it as occurring after the lokottara jñāna.
options_considered: āśraya as a generic support, body, or basis
āśraya as specifically sarvabījaka ālayavijñāna
parāvṛtti as simultaneous with the preceding predicates
parāvṛtti as following after the supramundane knowledge
justifications.chosen: āśraya = sarvabījaka ālayavijñāna; parāvṛtti follows that knowledge

evidence.quote:
tasya jñānasyānantaraṃ āśrayasya parāvṛttir bhavatīti jñāpanārtham āha
āśrayasya parāvṛttir iti
āśrayo 'tra sarvabījakam ālayavijñānam
evidence.translation: To indicate that after that knowledge the turning-about of the basis occurs, he says ‘the turning-about of the basis.’ Here ‘basis’ is the all-seed ālaya-vijñāna.

justifications.decision: Render parāvṛtti as Yogācāra ‘turning-about/transformation,’ not merely physical turning or reversal.
options_considered: parāvṛtti as simple turning back or reversal
parāvṛtti as a technical transformation of the basis
justifications.chosen: parāvṛtti is a transformative turning involving cessation of the old dispositions and presence of fitness, dharmakāya, and nondual knowledge

evidence.quote:
tasya parāvṛttir yā
dauṣṭhulyavipākadvayavāsanābhāvena nivṛttau satyāṃ
karmaṇyatādharmakāyādvayajñānabhāvena parāvṛttiḥ
evidence.translation: Its turning-about is the turning-about in which, when cessation has occurred through the absence of the latent impressions of the two—dauṣṭhulya and vipāka—there is the presence of fitness, dharma-body, and nondual knowledge.

justifications.decision: Construe dvidhā dauṣṭhulya-hānitaḥ as ‘through abandonment of the twofold dauṣṭhulya,’ not as a vague twofold abandonment or merely twofold parāvṛtti.
options_considered: dvidhā as modifying hāni: two abandonments
dvidhā as modifying parāvṛtti alone
dvidhā as specifying the two obstruction-dauṣṭhulyas whose abandonment yields the turning-about
justifications.chosen: dvidhā qualifies dauṣṭhulya: the twofold dauṣṭhulya of the kleśa- and jñeya-obstructions

evidence.quote:
dvidhādauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ
dvidheti kleśāvaraṇadauṣṭhulyaṃ jñeyāvaraṇadauṣṭhulyañ ca
dauṣṭhulyam
evidence.translation: ‘Through abandonment of twofold dauṣṭhulya.’ ‘Twofold’ means the dauṣṭhulya of the afflictive obstruction and the dauṣṭhulya of the obstruction concerning what is to be known.

evidence.quote: dvidhā āvaraṇabhedena sottarā niruttarā cāśrayaparāvṛttir uktā
evidence.translation: By the distinction of the obstructions, āśraya-parāvṛtti is said to be twofold: with a higher remainder and unsurpassed.

justifications.decision: Translate dauṣṭhulya with the technical sense ‘unwieldiness/unfitness,’ not merely ‘grossness’ or ‘badness.’
options_considered: dauṣṭhulya as ordinary coarseness/grossness
dauṣṭhulya as moral badness in general
dauṣṭhulya as technical unfitness of the basis, seed of the two obstructions
justifications.chosen: dauṣṭhulya = the basis’s akarmaṇyatā and the seeds of the two obstructions

evidence.quote:
dauṣṭhulyam
āśrayasyākarmaṇyatā
tat punaḥ kleśajñeyāvaraṇayor bījam
evidence.translation: Dauṣṭhulya is the basis’s lack of fitness/workability; that, again, is the seed of the afflictive and knowable-obstructions.

justifications.decision: Take hānitaḥ as ablatival-causal: the turning-about is obtained through the abandonment of the twofold dauṣṭhulya.
options_considered: hānitaḥ as a genitive ‘of loss’
hānitaḥ as a regular feminine ablative form hānyāḥ
hānitaḥ as an ablatival causal indeclinable: through/from abandonment

evidence.quote:
sā punar āśrayaparāvṛttiḥ kasya prahāṇāt prāpyate
ata āha
dvidhādauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ
evidence.translation: Again, by the abandoning of what is that turning-about of the basis obtained? Therefore he says: ‘through abandonment of twofold dauṣṭhulya.’

evidence.quote:
āśrayaparāvṛttiḥ śrāvakādigatadauṣṭhulyahānitaś ca prāpyate yad āha
vimuktikāya iti
bodhisattvagatadauṣṭhulyahānitaś ca prāpyate yad āha
dharmākhyo 'yaṃ mahāmuner iti
evidence.translation: Āśraya-parāvṛtti is obtained through abandonment of dauṣṭhulya belonging to śrāvakas and so on—hence ‘liberation-body’; and it is obtained through abandonment of dauṣṭhulya belonging to bodhisattvas—hence ‘this is called dharma [body] of the Great Sage.’

translation:
It is without grasping mind (acitta), non-apprehension (anupalamba);
and that is world-transcending knowledge (lokottara-jñāna).
Then comes the turning-about of the basis (āśraya-parāvṛtti),
through the abandonment of the twofold unwieldiness (dauṣṭhulya).
one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind translation might render acitta as “unconscious” and thereby deny jñāna; Sthiramati instead says grāhaka-citta is absent while the state is still lokottara jñāna.
It might take anupalamba as a vague failure of cognition; Sthiramati specifies non-apprehension of the grāhya-artha, the object-to-be-grasped.
It might translate lokottara as merely “extraordinary”; Sthiramati explains it as having crossed beyond the world because it is nirvikalpa and lacks worldly occurrence.
It might take āśraya as any support or the body; Sthiramati defines it here as the sarvabījaka ālayavijñāna.
It might construe dvidhā as twofold abandonment or twofold transformation in general; Sthiramati identifies two dauṣṭhulyas, those of the kleśa- and jñeya-obstructions.
It might read dauṣṭhulya as ordinary ‘grossness’; Sthiramati defines it technically as the basis’s akarmaṇyatā and as the seed of the two obstructions.
analyzer_disagreements: The analyzer’s api_seg gives dauṣṭhulya_hānyāḥ, but the supplied bhāṣya/local reading is dauṣṭhulya-hānitaḥ; I follow hānitaḥ.

ByT5 tags hānitaḥ as hāni_SBF. I analyze dauṣṭhulyahānitaḥ as an indeclinable tasil-derived ablatival form from the compound stem dauṣṭhulyahāni, since hānitaḥ is not the regular feminine i-stem ablative and Sthiramati glosses the relation with prahāṇāt/prāpyate.
ByT5 separates dauṣṭhulya as a compound member and hānitaḥ as the head; I combine them in the tatpuruṣa compound dauṣṭhulyahāni before the tasil ending.
The bhāṣya verse reading anupalambaḥ is retained morphologically; the commentary’s prose uses anupalambha-, so the translation follows that regular technical sense of non-apprehension.
